name: Tethys
description: Tethys, in Greek mythology, is a Titan goddess, daughter of Uranus (Heaven) and Gaia (Earth). She is primarily known as the personification of the primal font of fresh water, which nourished the earth and all living things. Tethys is often associated with the great rivers and springs of the world, embodying the life-giving and sustaining power of water. While less actively involved in the Olympian drama compared to other Titans, her significance lies in her primordial role as a foundational element of the cosmos. She is typically depicted as a serene and maternal figure, representing the vast, nurturing, and encompassing nature of the world's waters. Her influence extends to the very fabric of existence, as water was considered essential for life and fertility in ancient Greek cosmology. She is a primordial deity, representing a fundamental aspect of the cosmos before the rise of the Olympian gods. Tethys's role as a Titaness sets her apart from the later generation of deities, highlighting her connection to the ancient, foundational powers of the universe.
subtitle: Nurse of All

keyMyths.title: Tethys and Oceanus
keyMyths.description: Tethys, along with her brother and consort Oceanus, were believed to encircle the earth, representing the source of all rivers and seas. Their union symbolizes the continuous cycle of water, essential for life. They were the parents of numerous river gods and Oceanids (sea nymphs), highlighting their role as progenitors of the world's waterways and their associated deities. Their peaceful and harmonious existence contrasts with the conflicts that arose among the Olympian gods.

keyMyths.title: Tethys's Neutrality in the Titanomachy
keyMyths.description: During the Titanomachy, the great war between the Titans and the Olympian gods, Tethys and Oceanus chose not to side with either faction. This neutrality allowed them to maintain their vital role in sustaining the world. Their decision underscores their primary concern for the balance and continuity of the natural world, prioritizing the flow of water over political allegiances. This act also protected them from the wrath of Zeus.

keyMyths.title: Hera's upbringing by Tethys
keyMyths.description: According to some accounts, Hera, the queen of the Olympian gods, was raised by Tethys. This highlights Tethys's nurturing and maternal qualities, extending beyond her direct offspring. It also suggests a connection between the primordial Titaness and the Olympian pantheon, illustrating the continuity and evolution of the Greek mythological landscape. This role also emphasizes the importance of Tethys as a foundational figure who indirectly influenced the reign of the Olympian gods.
